post: Tomorrow is the last day of the Civic Star Challenge  Cycle 4 closes at midnght on the 15th. If your classroom had a moment worth remembering from America's 250th anniversary year, today is the day to share it. 1 entry. 1 final $300 drawing. 2 minutes between now and the close. To every teacher who entered this year: thank you. What happened in your classrooms during this historic school year matters, and the Bill of Rights Institute will be back in the fall with more opportunities to recognize the work you do every day. Last chance:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #America250  #CivicsEducation  #BillOfRightsInstitute

There's a difference between a government you disagree with and a government that eliminates your voice entirely. The colonists understood that distinction clearly by the 1770s. When colonial assemblies pushed back against British taxation, King George III didn't negotiate, he dissolved them. Massachusetts first, then North Carolina and Virginia, then any colony that moved toward organizing a Continental Congress. Grievance 5 of the Declaration of Independence marks a turning point: the moment colonists stopped seeing British policy as overreach and started seeing it as tyranny. The shutdown of representative government wasn't a political disagreement anymore. It was a crisis of sovereignty. Episode 5 of our 27-part series is live. Join Constitution Day Live →  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanRevolution  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

If the danger was going to come to our political institutions it would come from within. It would come from the alienation of people from their institutions and laws,  said host Matthew Brogdon to guest David Bobb of the Bill of Rights Institute on Season 3, Epsiode 19 of 𝙏𝙝𝙞𝙨 𝘾𝙤𝙣𝙨𝙩𝙞𝙩𝙪𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣. They discuss the importance of civic education and its impact in the United States today. Listen, http://bit.ly/4dLYuKY, or watch,  https://bit.ly/3BTJ5e7.

What does it take to undermine self-government without technically breaking the law? In Grievance 4, the colonists had an answer: move the meeting. Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG, Utah Valley University) returns to break down how royal governors used remote relocation orders to pressure colonial legislatures during moments of peak political tension, including Governor Gage forcing the Massachusetts Assembly out of Boston and into Salem in the lead-up to the Revolution. The tactic wasn't constant. But its occasional use was enough to demonstrate that colonial governance could be controlled from across the ocean. And for the colonists, the principle mattered as much as the pattern. Explore more:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #USHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #AmericanRevolution

"No taxation without representation" is famous. But representation was being denied long before the tax debates. Grievance 3 of the Declaration of Independence targets a quieter form of disenfranchisement: colonial districts created but denied any voice in their own legislatures. The policy, engineered by King George III and Lord Hillsborough, was designed to limit colonial expansion, and, with it, colonial political power. Jefferson had seen it clearly by 1774. Without representation, he wrote, Americans would be subject to "the absolute slaves of the king's sovereign will." By 1776, "Farmer George" had a new name. Zachary Deibel, assistant professor of history at VMI, unpacks Grievance 3 in Episode 3 of our 27-part series. Join Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanRevolution  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

The four-year college degree has long been treated as the default pathway to professional success. And for many students, it still is. Degree holders earn higher average lifetime incomes and face lower unemployment rates than those without a bachelor's degree. But tuition has risen sharply. Student loan debt has become a defining financial reality for millions of graduates. And the economy has opened meaningful alternative pathways  trade schools, apprenticeships, certifications, and direct workforce entry  that didn't exist at the same scale a generation ago. Think the Vote's final debate of the school year: Is a four-year college degree worth it today? Submissions close May 21. End strong. 👉  https://bit.ly/4tlNCv4  #CivicsEducation  #ThinktheVote  #CollegeDebt  #SocialStudies  #BRI  #CareerReadiness

The future of civic education gets reimagined in rooms like this one. Last week, the Civics Innovation Lab gathered educators, community leaders, and technology partners at the Museum of History and Industry in Seattle for an evening that was equal parts inspiration and action. The panelists and dinner conversations were insightful, and the Cocktail Innovation Lab didn't hurt either. What we heard from attendees confirmed what we've hearing from similar events around the country: the future of civics will be co-created together, through community engagement and local partners. Every community faces unique challenges, and so every community needs unique solutions. Language barriers, digital divides, and rural isolation look different both within Washington state and across the country. A one-size-fits-all approach won't solve inequitable access or civic disengagement. That's why the Civics Innovation Lab is working with local partners like we met in Seattle: actively listening to what diverse populations need, collaborating on community-first solutions, and helping young people break old paradigms that limit civic participation. If you believe the next generation deserves better civic education, and the tools to match, we'd love to have you in the conversation. 🔗  https://bit.ly/48ErdS4  #CivicEducation  #Civics  #EdTech  #CivicsInnovation  #Seattle  #BRI
